title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 1: The Great Depression (1929–1939)
hook: Nine thousand banks failed. Twenty-five percent unemployment. And the policies meant to stabilise it made it worse.
description: The Great Depression was not just a stock market crash — it was a systemic collapse that exposed every flaw in the relationship between governments and markets. This episode traces how the crash of 1929 triggered a cascade of bank failures, deflation, and global contagion that no existing policy framework could handle. It took the abandonment of the gold standard, the New Deal, and a decade of suffering before recovery arrived — and even then, it was the Second World War that finally ended mass unemployment.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — The Crash and Collapse
1:51  Problems — Four Interlocking Failures
3:10  Solutions — The New Deal and Monetary Break
4:55  Results — Recovery and the Keynesian Legacy
full_description:
Nine thousand banks failed. Twenty-five percent unemployment. And the policies meant to stabilise it made it worse.

The Great Depression was not just a stock market crash — it was a systemic collapse that exposed every flaw in the relationship between governments and markets. This episode traces how the crash of 1929 triggered a cascade of bank failures, deflation, and global contagion that no existing policy framework could handle. It took the abandonment of the gold standard, the New Deal, and a decade of suffering before recovery arrived — and even then, it was the Second World War that finally ended mass unemployment.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 2: The Stagflation Crisis (1973–1982)
hook: High inflation and high unemployment at the same time. The textbooks said it was impossible.
description: The 1970s stagflation crisis shattered the Keynesian consensus that had shaped economic policy since the Depression. Oil shocks, wage-price spirals, and the limits of demand management combined to produce a decade of economic stagnation that reshaped the entire field of macroeconomics. Paul Volcker's brutal interest rate cure worked — but at the cost of a severe recession and the political transformation of economic policymaking.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — Oil Shocks and the Phillips Curve Breaks
2:06  Problems — The Wage-Price Spiral
4:01  Solutions — Volcker's Shock Therapy
5:30  Results — Inflation Targeting and the Great Moderation
full_description:
High inflation and high unemployment at the same time. The textbooks said it was impossible.

The 1970s stagflation crisis shattered the Keynesian consensus that had shaped economic policy since the Depression. Oil shocks, wage-price spirals, and the limits of demand management combined to produce a decade of economic stagnation that reshaped the entire field of macroeconomics. Paul Volcker's brutal interest rate cure worked — but at the cost of a severe recession and the political transformation of economic policymaking.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 3: The Asian Financial Crisis (1997–1998)
hook: The Thai baht collapsed. Within months, currencies across East Asia were in freefall.
description: The 1997–1998 Asian financial crisis was a crisis of capital flows — rapid liberalisation without adequate regulatory infrastructure left emerging economies exposed to sudden reversals. What began as a currency crisis in Thailand spread to Indonesia, South Korea, Malaysia, and beyond, threatening the global financial system. The IMF's response was contested then and remains debated now; the lasting legacy was a fundamental reshaping of how developing nations manage their foreign reserves.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — Currency Pegs and Capital Flight
2:16  Problems — Contagion and Structural Vulnerabilities
3:50  Solutions — IMF Interventions and Crisis Management
5:20  Results — Reserves, Reform, and Unlearned Lessons
full_description:
The Thai baht collapsed. Within months, currencies across East Asia were in freefall.

The 1997–1998 Asian financial crisis was a crisis of capital flows — rapid liberalisation without adequate regulatory infrastructure left emerging economies exposed to sudden reversals. What began as a currency crisis in Thailand spread to Indonesia, South Korea, Malaysia, and beyond, threatening the global financial system. The IMF's response was contested then and remains debated now; the lasting legacy was a fundamental reshaping of how developing nations manage their foreign reserves.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 4: The Global Financial Crisis (2008–2012)
hook: In September 2008, the global financial system came within hours of complete collapse.
description: The 2008 Global Financial Crisis was the most severe economic shock since the Great Depression. Originating in the US mortgage market, it spread through complex financial instruments to bring down major institutions worldwide. The policy response — unprecedented in scale — prevented a depression but left a decade of slow recovery, rising inequality, and the political backlash that reshaped Western politics.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — Mortgage Bubble and Lehman's Fall
2:37  Problems — Systemic Risk and Regulatory Failure
4:15  Solutions — Bailouts, QE, and Fiscal Stimulus
5:50  Results — Recovery, Austerity, and Lasting Inequality
full_description:
In September 2008, the global financial system came within hours of complete collapse.

The 2008 Global Financial Crisis was the most severe economic shock since the Great Depression. Originating in the US mortgage market, it spread through complex financial instruments to bring down major institutions worldwide. The policy response — unprecedented in scale — prevented a depression but left a decade of slow recovery, rising inequality, and the political backlash that reshaped Western politics.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 5: The COVID-19 Economic Shock (2020)
hook: The fastest economic contraction in modern history — and the most rapid policy response ever deployed.
description: The COVID-19 economic shock of 2020 was unlike any previous crisis: deliberately induced, simultaneous across all sectors, and met with an unprecedented speed of fiscal and monetary response. The lessons drawn from 2008 meant governments acted in weeks rather than years — but also planted the seeds of the inflation crisis that followed. No crisis before it had combined a supply shock, a demand shock, and a deliberate government-mandated shutdown simultaneously.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — The Pandemic Shutdown
2:21  Problems — Supply, Demand, and the Policy Dilemma
3:59  Solutions — Furlough, Helicopter Money, and QE
5:44  Results — The Recovery, Inflation Seeds, and Scarring
full_description:
The fastest economic contraction in modern history — and the most rapid policy response ever deployed.

The COVID-19 economic shock of 2020 was unlike any previous crisis: deliberately induced, simultaneous across all sectors, and met with an unprecedented speed of fiscal and monetary response. The lessons drawn from 2008 meant governments acted in weeks rather than years — but also planted the seeds of the inflation crisis that followed. No crisis before it had combined a supply shock, a demand shock, and a deliberate government-mandated shutdown simultaneously.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 6: The Great Inflation (2021–2023)
hook: Inflation hadn't been a serious problem for 30 years. Then, almost everywhere at once, it came back.
description: The 2021–2023 inflation surge caught most central banks off guard — dismissed initially as transitory, it proved durable and broad-based. Supply chain disruption, energy price shocks, and the delayed effects of pandemic stimulus combined to produce the most aggressive rate-tightening cycle in a generation. Whether the soft landing succeeded or the full consequences have yet to arrive remains the defining open question of contemporary economics.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — Supply Shocks and the Return of Inflation
2:54  Problems — Transitory or Structural? The Forecasting Failure
4:49  Solutions — The Rate-Tightening Cycle
6:41  Results — Soft Landing, Recession, or Unfinished Business?
full_description:
Inflation hadn't been a serious problem for 30 years. Then, almost everywhere at once, it came back.

The 2021–2023 inflation surge caught most central banks off guard — dismissed initially as transitory, it proved durable and broad-based. Supply chain disruption, energy price shocks, and the delayed effects of pandemic stimulus combined to produce the most aggressive rate-tightening cycle in a generation. Whether the soft landing succeeded or the full consequences have yet to arrive remains the defining open question of contemporary economics.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 7: The Weimar Hyperinflation (1921–1923)
hook: Prices doubled every few days. Workers were paid twice a day. A wheelbarrow of cash couldn't buy a loaf of bread.
description: The Weimar hyperinflation of 1921–1923 remains the most extreme monetary collapse in a major industrial economy — and one of the most consequential. War reparations, political crisis, and the deliberate printing of money to pay government debts produced an inflation so extreme that the currency became worthless. The economic destruction contributed to the political instability that ultimately brought Hitler to power, making this one of history's most costly monetary policy failures.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — War Reparations and the Printing Press
2:00  Problems — Spiral Without a Floor
3:30  Solutions — The Rentenmark and Stabilisation
5:00  Results — Recovery and the Political Legacy
full_description:
Prices doubled every few days. Workers were paid twice a day. A wheelbarrow of cash couldn't buy a loaf of bread.

The Weimar hyperinflation of 1921–1923 remains the most extreme monetary collapse in a major industrial economy — and one of the most consequential. War reparations, political crisis, and the deliberate printing of money to pay government debts produced an inflation so extreme that the currency became worthless. The economic destruction contributed to the political instability that ultimately brought Hitler to power, making this one of history's most costly monetary policy failures.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 8: The Japanese Lost Decade (1990–2000)
hook: Japan was the future in 1989. By 1999, it had lost a decade — and never quite got it back.
description: The Japanese asset bubble of the 1980s inflated stocks and real estate to extraordinary valuations before collapsing in 1990. What followed was a decade of stagnation, deflation, and banking crisis that permanently reduced Japan's economic trajectory. The Bank of Japan's policy failures, the zombie bank problem, and the political economy of reform-avoidance became the template for every subsequent debate about how to escape a debt-deflation trap.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — The Asset Bubble and Its Collapse
2:15  Problems — Zombie Banks and Debt Deflation
4:00  Solutions — Quantitative Easing and Structural Reform
5:45  Results — Stagnation, Abenomics, and the Secular Stagnation Debate
full_description:
Japan was the future in 1989. By 1999, it had lost a decade — and never quite got it back.

The Japanese asset bubble of the 1980s inflated stocks and real estate to extraordinary valuations before collapsing in 1990. What followed was a decade of stagnation, deflation, and banking crisis that permanently reduced Japan's economic trajectory. The Bank of Japan's policy failures, the zombie bank problem, and the political economy of reform-avoidance became the template for every subsequent debate about how to escape a debt-deflation trap.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 9: The LTCM Collapse (1998)
hook: A hedge fund run by Nobel Prize winners nearly brought down the global financial system.
description: Long-Term Capital Management was the most sophisticated hedge fund in the world — staffed by two Nobel laureates and the architects of modern options pricing theory. In 1998, its mathematical models failed to account for the correlations that emerge in a crisis, and it lost four billion dollars in four months. The Federal Reserve orchestrated a bailout not to save the fund, but to prevent its twenty-five-to-one leveraged positions from triggering a global financial collapse.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — The Genius Trade and the Collapse
2:00  Problems — Leverage, Correlation, and Model Risk
3:30  Solutions — The Fed-Orchestrated Rescue
5:00  Results — Too Big to Fail and the Limits of Quantitative Finance
full_description:
A hedge fund run by Nobel Prize winners nearly brought down the global financial system.

Long-Term Capital Management was the most sophisticated hedge fund in the world — staffed by two Nobel laureates and the architects of modern options pricing theory. In 1998, its mathematical models failed to account for the correlations that emerge in a crisis, and it lost four billion dollars in four months. The Federal Reserve orchestrated a bailout not to save the fund, but to prevent its twenty-five-to-one leveraged positions from triggering a global financial collapse.

── CHAPTERS ──────────────────────────────

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 10: The Eurozone Debt Crisis (2010–2012)
hook: 'Whatever it takes.' Three words that ended a sovereign debt crisis threatening to break apart the euro.
description: The eurozone debt crisis exposed the fundamental design flaw of a monetary union without a fiscal union. When markets lost confidence in Greek, Irish, Portuguese, Spanish, and Italian sovereign debt, the ECB's inability and initial unwillingness to act as a lender of last resort turned a manageable debt problem into an existential threat to the euro project. Mario Draghi's three words — and the institution standing behind them — ultimately resolved what austerity and bailouts could not.

2:20  Problems — The Flawed Architecture of the Eurozone

full_description:
'Whatever it takes.' Three words that ended a sovereign debt crisis threatening to break apart the euro.

The eurozone debt crisis exposed the fundamental design flaw of a monetary union without a fiscal union. When markets lost confidence in Greek, Irish, Portuguese, Spanish, and Italian sovereign debt, the ECB's inability and initial unwillingness to act as a lender of last resort turned a manageable debt problem into an existential threat to the euro project. Mario Draghi's three words — and the institution standing behind them — ultimately resolved what austerity and bailouts could not.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 11: The Panic of 1907
hook: No central bank existed. One banker stepped in. And then Congress built the Federal Reserve.
description: The Panic of 1907 brought the American banking system to the edge of collapse — and there was no Federal Reserve to save it. J.P. Morgan, acting as a one-man central bank, orchestrated a private sector rescue that stabilised the system. But the crisis exposed an irreplaceable structural flaw: the world's largest economy had no lender of last resort. Six years later, the Federal Reserve Act was signed. The 1907 panic made it inevitable.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — Knickerbocker and the Bank Runs
2:00  Problems — No Lender of Last Resort
3:20  Solutions — J.P. Morgan's Private Rescue
4:45  Results — The Birth of the Federal Reserve
full_description:
No central bank existed. One banker stepped in. And then Congress built the Federal Reserve.

The Panic of 1907 brought the American banking system to the edge of collapse — and there was no Federal Reserve to save it. J.P. Morgan, acting as a one-man central bank, orchestrated a private sector rescue that stabilised the system. But the crisis exposed an irreplaceable structural flaw: the world's largest economy had no lender of last resort. Six years later, the Federal Reserve Act was signed. The 1907 panic made it inevitable.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 12: The Latin American Debt Crisis (1982–1989)
hook: Petrodollars flooded into Latin America. Then US interest rates spiked — and the entire region couldn't repay.
description: The Latin American debt crisis of the 1980s was the first systemic sovereign debt crisis of the modern era. Petrodollar recycling in the 1970s had loaded developing nations with variable-rate dollar debt; Volcker's rate hike made those debts unpayable overnight. Mexico's August 1982 announcement triggered a decade of austerity, economic stagnation, and political upheaval across the region — the 'lost decade' that reshaped development economics and created the Brady bond market.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — Petrodollars and the Volcker Shock
2:10  Problems — The Debt Trap and the IMF's Prescriptions
3:45  Solutions — The Baker Plan and the Brady Bonds
5:20  Results — Recovery and the Washington Consensus
full_description:
Petrodollars flooded into Latin America. Then US interest rates spiked — and the entire region couldn't repay.

The Latin American debt crisis of the 1980s was the first systemic sovereign debt crisis of the modern era. Petrodollar recycling in the 1970s had loaded developing nations with variable-rate dollar debt; Volcker's rate hike made those debts unpayable overnight. Mexico's August 1982 announcement triggered a decade of austerity, economic stagnation, and political upheaval across the region — the 'lost decade' that reshaped development economics and created the Brady bond market.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 13: Black Monday (1987)
hook: The largest single-day stock market crash in history. And the economy barely noticed.
description: On October 19, 1987, the Dow Jones fell twenty-two and a half percent in a single session — more than the crash of 1929. Portfolio insurance, program trading, and the disconnection between futures and cash markets created a mechanical spiral that no human could stop in time. The Federal Reserve's response — swift, credible, and targeted — prevented a financial market catastrophe from becoming an economic one. Black Monday established both the circuit breaker and the 'Greenspan put.'
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — The Mechanical Spiral
2:00  Problems — Portfolio Insurance and Market Structure
3:15  Solutions — The Fed's Liquidity Pledge
4:30  Results — Circuit Breakers and the Moral Hazard Legacy
full_description:
The largest single-day stock market crash in history. And the economy barely noticed.

On October 19, 1987, the Dow Jones fell twenty-two and a half percent in a single session — more than the crash of 1929. Portfolio insurance, program trading, and the disconnection between futures and cash markets created a mechanical spiral that no human could stop in time. The Federal Reserve's response — swift, credible, and targeted — prevented a financial market catastrophe from becoming an economic one. Black Monday established both the circuit breaker and the 'Greenspan put.'

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 14: The Dot-com Crash (2000–2001)
hook: Trillion-dollar companies with no revenue. Analysts lying to their clients. And it all collapsed in eighteen months.
description: The dot-com crash of 2000–2001 erased five trillion dollars in market value and exposed the conflicts of interest that had corrupted investment banking and equity research throughout the 1990s boom. The internet was genuinely transformative — but the valuations of 1999 disconnected from any plausible business model. When the Fed cut rates to limit the recession, it created the cheap money that inflated the next bubble: housing.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — The NASDAQ Mania and the Peak
2:05  Problems — Conflicts of Interest and the Valuation Delusion
3:30  Solutions — Rate Cuts, Sarbanes-Oxley, and the Research Settlement
5:00  Results — The Surviving Giants and the Next Bubble
full_description:
Trillion-dollar companies with no revenue. Analysts lying to their clients. And it all collapsed in eighteen months.

The dot-com crash of 2000–2001 erased five trillion dollars in market value and exposed the conflicts of interest that had corrupted investment banking and equity research throughout the 1990s boom. The internet was genuinely transformative — but the valuations of 1999 disconnected from any plausible business model. When the Fed cut rates to limit the recession, it created the cheap money that inflated the next bubble: housing.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 15: The Argentine Crisis (2001–2002)
hook: Five presidents in ten days. Bank deposits frozen. And the largest sovereign default in history.
description: Argentina's 2001–2002 crisis was the spectacular failure of convertibility — a currency board that pegged the peso one-to-one to the dollar. When external shocks made the peg unviable, the only adjustment mechanism was deflation and unemployment. The resulting collapse — default, devaluation, banking system failure, and mass poverty — was catastrophic. Yet the recovery that followed was rapid, raising fundamental questions about whether default is always the catastrophe that creditors claim.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — The Corralito and the Default
2:10  Problems — The Impossible Trinity and the Peg's Fatal Flaw
3:35  Solutions — Default, Devaluation, and Debt Restructuring
5:10  Results — The Rapid Recovery and the Vulture Fund Problem
full_description:
Five presidents in ten days. Bank deposits frozen. And the largest sovereign default in history.

Argentina's 2001–2002 crisis was the spectacular failure of convertibility — a currency board that pegged the peso one-to-one to the dollar. When external shocks made the peg unviable, the only adjustment mechanism was deflation and unemployment. The resulting collapse — default, devaluation, banking system failure, and mass poverty — was catastrophic. Yet the recovery that followed was rapid, raising fundamental questions about whether default is always the catastrophe that creditors claim.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 16: The Long Depression (1873–1896)
hook: Twenty years of deflation. And no government had any idea how to stop it.
description: The Long Depression of 1873–1896 was the first global financial crisis — simultaneous contractions in the United States, Britain, and Central Europe triggered by railroad speculation and the deflationary logic of the gold standard. It lasted two decades, reshaped industrial capitalism, and generated the political movements that would eventually produce the Federal Reserve, progressive taxation, and the regulatory state. No government had the tools to end it. The gold supply eventually did.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — Railroad Speculation and the Global Crash
2:10  Problems — Deflation, Debt, and No Policy Tools
3:35  Solutions — Tariffs, Gold Discovery, and Technological Absorption
5:00  Results — Industrial Concentration and the Progressive Era
full_description:
Twenty years of deflation. And no government had any idea how to stop it.

The Long Depression of 1873–1896 was the first global financial crisis — simultaneous contractions in the United States, Britain, and Central Europe triggered by railroad speculation and the deflationary logic of the gold standard. It lasted two decades, reshaped industrial capitalism, and generated the political movements that would eventually produce the Federal Reserve, progressive taxation, and the regulatory state. No government had the tools to end it. The gold supply eventually did.

title: Economic Crises Explained — Episode 17: The South Sea Bubble (1720)
hook: One company, backed by Parliament, promised to manage the national debt — and drove investors to ruin.
description: The South Sea Bubble of 1720 is the archetype of speculative mania: a joint-stock company with government backing, rising share prices disconnected from any underlying value, a collapse that ruined thousands, and a parliamentary scandal that implicated the highest levels of government. Three centuries later, the pattern — genuine innovation, speculative excess, collapse, and regulatory reform — remains the template for every financial bubble that followed.
chapters:
0:00  Introduction
0:47  Event — The Debt-for-Equity Scheme and the Mania
2:10  Problems — Fraud, Leverage, and No Disclosure Framework
3:35  Solutions — The Bubble Act and Walpole's Compromise
5:00  Results — The Corporate Law Legacy and the Template for All Bubbles
full_description:
One company, backed by Parliament, promised to manage the national debt — and drove investors to ruin.

The South Sea Bubble of 1720 is the archetype of speculative mania: a joint-stock company with government backing, rising share prices disconnected from any underlying value, a collapse that ruined thousands, and a parliamentary scandal that implicated the highest levels of government. Three centuries later, the pattern — genuine innovation, speculative excess, collapse, and regulatory reform — remains the template for every financial bubble that followed.
